A "Guide" for Anyone Who Wants to Deceive Their Employer.

Read this carefully — especially if you're the one responsible for making sure it doesn't happen.

Most fraud doesn't announce itself.
It doesn't arrive with red flags or warning signs. It walks through your front door as a new hire. It sits at a desk, attends your meetings, and quietly takes more than it gives.
Here are five ways people deceive employers — some during hiring, some from the inside. Most leave no digital footprint. Most require a trained eye to detect.

1. Reframing routine work as strategic leadership on a resume — a candidate who spent two years updating spreadsheets describes themselves as having "led data-driven financial transformation initiatives across the organization." Same experience. Different vocabulary. Entirely different hire.

2. Providing a personal contact as a professional reference — listed under a fabricated name, title, and company. The call goes smoothly. The feedback is glowing. The candidate moves forward. Nobody checks whether the number belongs to who it claims to.

3. Using AI in real time during video interviews to answer technical questions convincingly — with no preparation, no expertise, and no way for a standard interviewer to know.

4. Arranging undisclosed side payments with suppliers or vendors. A procurement manager steers contracts toward a specific vendor — not because of price or quality, but because of a private agreement made over a dinner that never appeared in any email. The vendor wins. The manager earns. The organization pays more than it should, and never knows why.

5. Falsifying expense reports in amounts small enough not to trigger immediate review — modest individually, significant over time, and remarkably difficult to catch without the right framework.

These aren't sophisticated schemes. They're quiet, practical, and far more common than most organizations realize.
The real problem isn't that people attempt them. It's that most organizations still rely on surface-level checks and basic trust.

A polished resume passes. A friendly reference confirms. Expense reports look clean.
And by the time the damage is visible — in lost margins, exposed data, or broken culture — the cost has already been paid.

The question isn't whether these risks exist inside your organization.
At any scale — from a growing mid-size business to a large enterprise — a single undetected hire or an undisclosed internal arrangement can quietly reshape your financial position, your reputation, and your culture in ways that take years to reverse.

The question is whether you have the intelligence layer to surface them before they compound.

In 2026, trust without verification isn't optimism. It's exposure.

P.R.D.S - Business Intelligence goes beyond the checkbox. Deep OSINT, behavioral profiling, pattern analysis, and forensic-level review — applied before a decision is made, or when something already doesn't feel right.

I didn’t plan for 2025 to look like this.

After my Sun Life contract ended, I made a deliberate decision to step outside the delivery cycle — balancing senior care responsibilities while also taking the opportunity to zoom out and reassess where financial services is actually heading.

What I found on the ground — particularly across the UAE — challenged a lot of assumptions.

In conversations with senior leaders and through active engagement in the region (including progressing through advanced-stage hiring processes), one theme kept coming up:

* The bottleneck is no longer understanding regulation.
* It’s operationalizing it — fast, at scale, and across fragmented systems.

That gap is where most transformation efforts are quietly failing.

During this time, I:

- Presented at AIM Congress (Abu Dhabi) on AI in Governance and Machine-Readable Compliance
- Engaged with fintech and RegTech ecosystems across DIFC
- Spent time analyzing how GCC institutions are approaching operational resilience, data strategy, and regulatory change

One insight stood out clearly:

Whether you’re looking at OSFI/IIROC or GCC regulators —
the challenge is global:

Turning regulatory intent into executable, data-driven systems.

Not documents. Not slide decks.
Systems that actually work.

That’s where I operate best — at the intersection of:

- Regulation
- Data
- Execution

And increasingly, how these are being reshaped by AI-driven approaches.

I’m now exploring where this perspective can create the most impact — particularly across regulatory transformation, capital markets modernization, and AI-enabled compliance.

If you’re working on similar challenges, I’d welcome the conversation.

Most people using Claude are still treating it like a chatbot. That's like hiring a surgeon to hand out bandaids.

Anthropic just made most of them irrelevant in a single afternoon.

On January 12, Claude Cowork quietly launched a desktop AI agent that sits inside your workflow. No fanfare. Then on January 30, Anthropic dropped 11 open-source plugins on GitHub

Contract risk review (bye LegalZoom)
Prospect research + outreach (bye half your sales stack)
Financial modeling (bye $99/month SaaS tools)
Customer support automation
A meta-plugin to build your own
The market noticed. $285 billion in SaaS market value erased in 72 hours. Atlassian down 21%. Adobe, Salesforce, ServiceNow all hit. JPMorgan's software index dropped 7% in days.
Then it got stranger.

The Pentagon demanded Anthropic lift its ethics guardrails for autonomous weapons and mass surveillance. Anthropic said no. Trump ordered federal agencies to stop using their tech. The Pentagon labeled them a "supply chain risk" , a designation previously reserved for Huawei.
The day after the federal ban, Claude hit #1 on the US App Store.

Daily signups crossed 1 million. DAUs hit 11.3M up 183% from January. Paid subscriptions doubled. 40 scientists from OpenAI and Google DeepMind filed an amicus brief defending Anthropic in court.

The company that refused the Pentagon became the most downloaded app in the country.

Two things happened simultaneously in Q1 2026:
1. The SaaS business model got structurally challenged in a way it hasn't been before.
2. Holding an ethical line in public turned out to be the best growth strategy anyone ran this quarter.
If you're still paying per seat for tools Claude can now replace it's time to audit your stack.

If you scan Anthropic’s careers page, you don’t see a lab quietly replacing humans; you see a full-stack enterprise software company racing to industrialise a new general-purpose capability, with dense hiring not just in research and infrastructure but in sales, finance, policy, safeguards and customer‑facing engineering. That apparent paradox - hiring salespeople, ops leaders and engineers while building tools that automate chunks of their work - is exactly what you’d expect when a technology shifts the production possibility frontier rather than simply cutting costs. 

The value isn’t “no salesperson” or “no programmer”; it is a smaller cohort of very high‑leverage people, each operating with an AI exoskeleton that lets them prosecute far more surface area than was humanly possible: engineers shipping and maintaining systems of previously unmanageable complexity, sales teams running deeply bespoke outreach and implementation plays at massive scale, support and success functions managing far larger books of business without losing fidelity. 

To make that work, you need humans who can orchestrate the tools, design workflows, enforce governance, negotiate contracts, manage risk, and translate messy organisational and regulatory constraints into something a model can actually optimise against. 

You also need category‑defining go‑to‑market and partnerships talent to wire these systems into existing enterprises, plus safeguards, security and policy teams to keep the whole thing within acceptable risk bounds. 

So the hiring pattern is a tell: we are not automating people out of the loop; we are rebuilding the loop so that the limiting factor becomes judgment, strategy and relationship‑building, while the models handle an ever‑growing share of the mechanical throughput.

The layoff wave in Web3 is brutal but the hiring patterns tell a different story

If you only read the headlines you'd think Web3 is shutting down entirely. 

But if you look at what's actually being hired for right now, a very different picture emerges. The industry isn't dying, it's restructuring & this tells you exactly where value is heading.

==> What's being cut

→ The 5-person marketing department running the same KOL & airdrop playbook from 2021 is being replaced by 1-2 people with AI tools & a strategy that ties to revenue.

→ 8 community managers across Discord, Telegram and Twitter is becoming 2-3 people with automation handling the routine work.

→ The "partnerships" role that produced logos on a website but no measurable business impact is gone. Every BD role now requires revenue attribution.

→ Multiple teams building overlapping features with no coordination. Bear markets force the question: do we actually need 30 engineers or do 12 focused ones ship faster?

→ Co-founders with vague operational roles, Chief Evangelist titles, advisory positions with compensation but no deliverables. The org chart is getting honest.

==> What's being quietly hired for

→ Every jurisdiction is building crypto frameworks simultaneously (MiCA, GENIUS Act, VASP Acts across Asia and Africa). Projects need people who understand this & most don't have them.

→ AI-integrated operations roles. Not "AI engineers" but operators who use AI to do the work of multiple people. 

→ Revenue-focused roles: Head of Revenue, Growth Lead with P&L responsibility, & Partnerships Manager with quota. Every title now has a number attached to it.

→ The people building the systems everything else runs on are not getting but raises. Security auditors and smart contract specialists are commanding premium compensation because one exploit destroys years of work.

→ As projects focus on specific geographies instead of "global" strategies, people who actually understand SSA, SEA, MENA or LATAM markets are in demand.

==> If you've been laid off here's the playbook

→ Don't panic-apply to 50 generic roles, reposition first. Ask yourself: what did I actually deliver that I can prove with data? Lead with outcomes.

→ Spend two weeks learning to integrate AI into whatever you do. 

→ If your old role doesn't exist anymore, don't look for the same title, look for the evolved version of what you did.

→ Consider emerging markets: Projects expanding into Africa, Southeast Asia & LATAM need people who understand those regions. If you have that knowledge, it's your competitive edge right now.

→ Build in public while you search. The person who's visibly adding value while between roles gets hired faster than the one sending silent applications.

Web3 is shedding roles built for a market that no longer exists and creating roles built for the market that's forming. Ensure you're positioned for the second category.

If you've been affected and want to talk about repositioning, reach out.

LLM development isn't "AI writes code"; it's a model shift: you're no longer a programmer, you manage the system through requirements, constraints, and tests. Code is now a side effect. The basic cycle is: Epic → contract → generation → tests → deploy. The key is to not give LLM freedom: prompt = contract (GOAL / CONSTRAINTS / DONE_WHEN), rules = hardcoded architecture, tests = sole control. Planning is done through domains and aggregates, not files—otherwise, you'll ruin the system. You don't read PRs anymore—you check behavior. Errors will increase—that means you need fallbacks, feature flags, and stranglers to prevent them from affecting the user. Bottleneck is shifting: we used to write code, now we manage requirements and tests. In short: manage the input, not the output, otherwise you'll be fixing the endless garbage you generated yourself.

I have bad news and a cold shower for you.

If you're still coding like it's 2019, I have bad news for you. While you're arguing about tabs vs. spaces, guys are already writing services by talking to AI. Claude Code isn't a "helper"; it's an intern-junior-middle-semi-team all at once. It works fast, sometimes it's slow, sometimes it's surprising. But most importantly, it speeds up development so much that the traditional "team → sprint → release" model starts to look like dial-up internet.

How to get started:
- Go to the Anthropic website (https://lnkd.in/gDgEETzG)
- Download Claude Code
- Open it and log in
- Message it:
Make a hello world with a front-end and back-end, so I can understand how you work and appreciate your power
- Wait 5 minutes
- Look at what it generated, and understand: this is just the beginning

Now, a cold shower

There will be more errors. If in classic development the noise level of production errors is ~2%, then here it's easily 5-6% plus the risk of incidents. Why? Because the generation speed is growing faster than our monitoring tools. This is a normal price for speedup. You just need to understand the limitations, maintain monitoring, alerts, and not have illusions that AI writes perfect code.

Another unexpected effect. When one person can generate as much code as an entire team, the classic "10 branches → 10 PRs → 10 conflicts → endless merges" model becomes a pain. I don't know what to do about it yet, and it seems few people know...

And the speedup—no joke. A very rough estimate: 10 x 7 ≈ 70 times. What a team of 7 used to do in a 2-week (10-day) sprint can now be done almost single-handedly. Not because you've become a genius, but because there's an endlessly patient code generator sitting next to you.

News for those who wrote code "by feel." If you don't understand units and integration tests, don't know how to divide a system into domains, and don't understand context boundaries—you'll find yourself in a long and painful hell. Because AI writes code perfectly, but you have to maintain the architecture. And yes, architectural tests, contract tests, automated domain boundary checks, and a bunch of other fun stuff are already appearing.

So the new developer skill sounds simple: become an architect. Everyone wanted to be one, right? Here's your chance. Because a developer who manages AI through architecture will be worth as much as a team. And someone who simply writes functions will be arguing with autocomplete while the market races ahead.

Thoughts after visiting Anthropic’s San Francisco office last week:

The speed at which AI is advancing is staggering—and Germany is falling behind.

My visit to #Anthropic last week was eye-opening. They’ve doubled their workforce in a year and are hiring 150 people per week. Anthropic has a $380billion enterprise evaluation and just raised $30billion in a series G funding round. Since the recent policy shift around US government contracts, #Claude has hit record user #growth—reports suggest up to 1mio. new users daily.

So what does this mean for traditional German manufacturers?

We need to adapt fast. Companies must embrace AI-first thinking and build #agentic #workflows into their organization. This is happening now—not in five years.

And the typewriter? 🎹

At Anthropic, they had this beautiful installation: a vintage typewriter where you could write a prompt, feed it to Claude, and watch the AI’s response print out. #Innovation meeting #tradition. It perfectly captures the moment we’re in.

The real question for Europe: Are we moving fast enough?

What’s your take? How are businesses in your region adapting to the AI revolution?

The real AI conversation has shifted - from possibility to implementation, from hype to judgment, and from technology alone to people as well.

We didn't talk about the "Why" of AI. We didn’t need to. At the WomenExecs on Boards #APACSummit this week, I grounded my panel with Tan Su Shan (CEO, DBS Bank) and Anu Madgavkar (Partner, McKinsey Global Institute) firmly in the reality of implementation.

We stayed in the weeds, then zoomed out to the macro, exploring the uncomfortable spaces opening up between #macro_necessity and #micro_reality.

1. The Practitioner's Reality: Implementation as the Path 
Su Shan was remarkably candid about the friction of moving from "experimenting" to "production." She described a world where transformation isn't a smooth line - it involves starts, halts, and course corrections. This isn’t a sign of failure; it’s a sign that something real is being attempted. The core challenge for a Board is bridging the "VELOCITY MISMATCH": if technology moves in weekly sprints and Governance moves in 90-day cycles, the Board becomes a bottleneck, not a steward.

2. The Macro-Economic Mandate & "Unassociated Risks" Anu zoomed us out to the stark demographic reality: with a shrinking global workforce, AI is no longer a luxury-it is a fundamental requirement for productivity. But she also issued a warning about UNASSOCIATED RISKS-those second-order systemic effects, like shifts in social contracts and labor market stability, that boards often leave unexamined while focusing narrowly on project-level ROI.

3. The Structural Shift: The "Diamond-Shaped" Organization This tension is hollowing out the entry-level-the traditional base of the organizational pyramid. We are seeing the emergence of a "Diamond" structure: fat in the middle with experienced practitioners, but thin at the bottom. For a Board, this is a massive TALENT PIPELINE RISK. How do we build the leaders of 2035 if we aren't hiring the juniors of 2026?

THE BOARD'S ROLE: PROVIDING "COVER" Management cannot scale if the Board remains risk-averse.  Success requires "Board Cover" -the trust and strategic engagement to adapt, stop, and restart at the pace the technology now demands.

MY TAKEAWAY: AI transformation isn't just scaling software; it’s building the organizational capacity to navigate a moving target without losing judgment or nerve. This isn't about less governance; it’s about INTENTIONAL DYNAMIC GOVERNANCE - stewarding the "soul" of the company while the structure beneath it changes forever.

A heartfelt thank you to Judy LEE and Christine Fellowes for leading this event with such grace and intention. 

Grateful to my fellow WEOB members who worked tirelessly behind the scenes to manage the complexity of this event. It is a privilege to be part of this community. Anamika Talwar Sakaya Johns Rani Lisa Johnson Margaret Ma Connolly Nina Altarac  Eleonore Dachicourt.

You shouldn’t need 5 tools to complete one task.

But that’s exactly what most businesses are doing right now.

Open one tab to research. Another to write. Another to organise.

Then copy, paste, adjust, and repeat.

It adds up.

Not just in time, but in energy, focus, and consistency.
And over time, it becomes the way your business operates.
Manual. Fragmented. Slower than it should be.

This is where things need to change.

Instead of stitching tools together, imagine having AI agents that handle the flow of work with you.
That’s what ProPrompt is built for.

It gives you a business suite of AI agents that sit alongside you in your browser, so when you’re working on something, you’re not starting from scratch or jumping between platforms.
